questions.prompt: A developer built a custom card input form on your team's site, sending card numbers to your backend before forwarding to Stripe. A compliance audit flags this. Why?

choices.text: Sending card numbers through your server makes you in-scope for PCI-DSS — a heavy compliance burden. Stripe Elements solves this by keeping card data on the client and tokenizing it via Stripe.js before it ever touches your server. Migrate to Elements; use Appearance API to match the team's design while keeping PCI scope minimal.

questions.explanation: Receiving raw card data on your server triggers full PCI-DSS scope — expensive audits, ongoing compliance burden. Stripe Elements is the canonical solution: card data goes directly from browser to Stripe; your server never touches it. Combine with Appearance API for custom design.

questions.prompt: What does the Express Checkout Element offer that the regular Payment Element doesn't?

choices.text: Renders dedicated one-tap wallet buttons (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Link) at the top of your checkout. Users tap and pay without filling a form. Higher conversion on mobile especially. Works alongside the Payment Element (which handles cards entered manually).

questions.explanation: Express Checkout Element surfaces wallet/express options prominently; users skip typing card details. On mobile especially, conversion bumps can be substantial. Use both Express Checkout (top of form for wallets) + Payment Element (below for typed cards).
